narrative_blocks.header: Born without a roof to his mouth
narrative_blocks.detail: His principal crop was rye; he spelled it 'ri.' His carefully kept account book, dated January 1, 1762, survived and was published in Caroline Newton's family history. The speech impediment was not only a social burden — it shaped his entire Revolutionary War service. Strangers could not understand him, and when he could not give the countersign intelligibly, he risked being shot by his own side. When Britain declared war with France and the colonial militia faced possible service in Canada, his Captain consulted a higher officer, who granted Burwell an exemption from duty. The document of exemption was preserved in the family. (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914)
narrative_blocks.body: When the children in the little schoolhouse on Phillida Hill laughed at his cleft-palate speech, Burwell refused to attend. He taught himself to read, write, and keep accounts, his spelling defiantly phonetic — if he chose to spell his principal crop “ri,” that was the best way to spell it.

narrative_blocks.header: Defended Kingston and the Continental Congress in 1777
narrative_blocks.detail: His service in both deployments was brief — not from cowardice but from the practical impossibility of the countersign problem. His unit could not rely on him for sentry duty or night patrol, since any stranger who challenged him could not understand his response. He served with what he could offer and was released. He is on the DAR lineage rolls through his descendant Miss Sarah Newton Church (NSDAR No. 99934; DAR Lineage Book 100:291). (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1777 Burwell was at Kingston, New York, when the Continental Congress was assembled there under threat from British troops. In 1779 he enlisted in Capt. Charles Norton's Company, Col. Thaddeus Cook's regiment, and was called to New Haven and Fairfield during the British coastal raids of July 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: His stammer nearly kept him from war
narrative_blocks.body: Born without a roof to his mouth, Burwell Newton could not give a military countersign clearly enough for strangers to understand — a danger that could get a sentry shot. When Britain’s war with France threatened to send the militia to Canada, Col. Timothy Stone authorized his discharge, writing that officers had “full Power to Relieve any Soldier… unfit for service.”

military_service.notes: At the defence of Kingston, New York, 1777, when Continental Congress was assembled there and threatened by British troops. Called to New Haven and Fairfield in July 1779. Did not remain long in service — his speech impediment made it dangerous: strangers could not understand him and he risked being shot when unable to give the countersign intelligibly.

bio.bio_blurb: Durham farmer who soldiered despite a cleft palate

bio.bio_blurb: Durham farmer who soldiered despite a cleft palate
Descendant of two men burned by frontier raiders

narrative_blocks.header: She left seven children and a newborn
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Johnson married Burwell Newton at nineteen and bore eight children before dying at thirty-eight, weeks after her last son Isaac was born. With no mother and a father who never remarried, the children kept house themselves — the eldest seventeen, the youngest daughters eleven and eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of her forebears were burned by raiders
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice descended from a family marked by frontier violence: her great-great-grandfather Capt. John Plympton was captured and burned at the stake near Deerfield in 1675 during King Philip’s War, and her great-grandfather Nathaniel Sutliff met the same fate the following year. The orphaned Sutliff children received a grant of land for their support.

bio.bio_blurb: Durham landowner who funded a district school

narrative_blocks.header: He guarded his gold with horse-pistols
narrative_blocks.body: Burwell Newton Jr. accumulated considerable property and kept his money in a great chest beside his bed, guarded by a pair of horse-pistols. When a kinsman came to borrow, several hundred dollars in gold were counted out from it.

narrative_blocks.header: He stood through every long Sabbath prayer
narrative_blocks.body: A devoted reader who made faithful use of the Durham Library — the first public library in Connecticut, founded 1733 — Burwell kept the old customs to the end. As men had done in his youth, he stood through the long meeting-house prayer each Sabbath until he was nearly ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: He prosecuted neighbors over the Sabbath
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained deacon of Durham’s First Congregational Church in 1827, Samuel Newton was appointed the officer charged with prosecuting Seventh-day Baptists for not observing the Christian Sabbath. He led the Durham Sunday School Association until his death and sat in the General Assembly in 1856.

narrative_blocks.header: His birthday verses warned of the grave
narrative_blocks.body: A writer of verses, Samuel marked his daughter Eliza’s eighth birthday with a poem warning that before eight more years passed, her “head may lie low in the dust.” She lived instead to seventy, teaching school and remembering the church in her will.

narrative_blocks.header: He praised five brothers who never quarreled
narrative_blocks.body: A merchant in the South, Durham, and Middletown who ended his days in Jersey City, John Newton wrote admiringly of his father’s generation — the five brothers Abiathar, Burwell, Abner, Roger, and Isaac — saying he never knew so upright a band of brothers, among whom no word of censure ever passed.

narrative_blocks.header: He marched to New Haven at fourteen
narrative_blocks.body: Abner Newton enlisted before he was fifteen and marched with his company to New Haven in July 1779 when the British invaded under General Tryon. The heavy flintlock musket he carried was kept until his death in 1852, when his grandsons destroyed it.

narrative_blocks.header: “Their guns shone like silver”
narrative_blocks.body: In extreme old age Abner relived his war years, pointing to a hill near his home and saying he had watched the British come over it, their guns shining like silver — and that he was glad to see them go. He had stood guard the night Arnold burned New London.

narrative_blocks.header: He refused a pension on principle
narrative_blocks.body: Though his comrades applied for pensions, Abner would not, saying he did not wish his townsmen to remark when he died, “Another old pensioner gone.” He was ordained deacon some twenty-five years after his boyhood service.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father died saving smallpox prisoners
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Fairchild’s father Elisha drove his team to Milford in January 1777 to carry home Continental soldiers released sick and dying from British prison ships. He caught smallpox from those he rescued and died within three weeks, leaving his widow with five young children.

narrative_blocks.header: Her wedding dress was fine linen chintz
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Fairchild married Abner Newton in 1788 in a wedding dress of fine linen chintz, having survived a hard childhood after her father’s death left her mother a widow at thirty-one with five small children. The couple lived thirteen years in Hartland before returning to Durham.

cross_connections.display_label: whose son married his daughter, uniting two Newton lines

narrative_blocks.header: Forty years a deacon, a winter schoolmaster
narrative_blocks.body: Gaylord Newton farmed, taught the district school in winters, served as militia captain, selectman, and assessor, and was for forty years a deacon of Durham’s First Congregational Church. His son Henry Gleason would carry the family name into Connecticut law.

narrative_blocks.header: He once left a day’s clams behind
narrative_blocks.body: A cousin’s fond letter recalled Gaylord spending a whole day gathering clams at Long Pond, only to drive home and realize — on seeing the water — that he had left every clam behind. It was offered as a gentle emblem of how memory fades with age.

cross_connections.display_label: whose daughter married his son, uniting two Newton lines

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Welch Fowler, second wife of Samuel Newton

marriage_date.notes: Married early 1724; deed dated June 18, 1724 gives "Abner Newton of Milford and Mary his wife"

primary_residence.notes: Removed from Milford to Durham, settling there in late 1724. Deed of July 1724 calls him "Abner Newton of Milford"; deed of December 1724 calls him "Abner Newton, late of Milford, now of Durham."

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother shared same first name, Abner
narrative_blocks.body: This Abner Newton was born 1699 to Samuel Newton's second wife Sarah Welch Fowler — distinguishing him from his half-brother Abner Newton baptized May 1689, who had been born to Samuel's first wife Martha Fenn ten years earlier. The repeated Abner name across both marriages was uncommon. The 1699 Abner became the documented ancestor of the Durham Newton branch — earning the 'durham_newton' tag in this entry.

narrative_blocks.header: 1724 Milford-to-Durham move; wife sold inherited share
narrative_blocks.body: In 1724 Abner Newton and Mary Burwell removed from Milford to Durham Connecticut — and on the move Mary sold her own inherited property, a quarter share of a dwelling house and home-lot in Milford, to Susannah Burwell (widow of John Burwell) for eighty pounds. Per the deed boundaries this was the homestead of the first John Burwell, occupied since 1639. Mary had kept a share in her family's original Milford homestead until the day she left for Durham.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Roger received Alice Newton's Yale bequest
narrative_blocks.body: Abner's son Roger Newton was the beneficiary of Alice Newton's ca.1740 conditional Yale bequest — establishing the family's Yale-funded ministerial track. Roger graduated Yale 1758 and served fifty-six years as minister at Greenfield Massachusetts until 1816 — one of the longest continuous American pastorates of the Revolutionary-Republican era.

burial.plot_notes: Oldest part of the Milford Cemetery. FindAGrave #29848117.

cross_connections.display_label: Their wives were half-sisters — Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Militia ensign in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: When King Philip's War broke out in 1675, Samuel was appointed ensign in the Milford militia at roughly age 29. He served through the war and its recurring aftermath of Indian skirmishes, rising to captain of the Milford militia by 1698.

narrative_blocks.header: Represented Milford fifteen times in the General Court
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Milford in fifteen sessions of the Connecticut General Court between 1691 and 1703 — a span of sustained civic service that ran alongside his captaincy of the militia. His father Roger Newton Sr. had helped found the town's church.

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker left him £10 in his will
narrative_blocks.body: He was nine months old when his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker died in 1647. Thomas's will left a £10 legacy to him — a substantial inheritance for a Hooker grandchild born during the Hartford founding.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife and cousin's wife were Fenn half-sisters
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Martha Fenn's half-sister Susanna married William Hooker — Samuel's first cousin. The Fenn family thus connected to the Hooker tree twice in the same generation through different lines.

narrative_blocks.header: A second marriage to a soldier's widow
narrative_blocks.body: After Martha Fenn died, Samuel married Sarah Welch Fowler, widow of John Fowler, who had been killed taking an Indian fort in 1693. Their son Abner bore a name found nowhere else in the Newton or Hooker line.

career.notes: Represented Milford in 15 sessions of the General Court, 1691-1703.

military_service.notes: Appointed ensign 1675 at outbreak of King Philip's War. Served in recurring skirmishes with Indians. Rose to captain of Milford militia 1698. Source: Newton Genealogy (Leonard 1915).

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Welch of Milford — one of the Seven Pillars of the Milford church

crossConnections.display_label: a great-great-grandson who overcame the same speech difficulty
